metadata.career_relevance: Flipkart's Spring Boot backend team needed to add a GraphQL API alongside their existing REST endpoints without a full rewrite. Spring for GraphQL: add one starter, put the schema file in resources/graphql/, annotate methods with @QueryMapping and @MutationMapping — existing Spring Boot patterns, zero new learning curve. After this topic, you can add a production-ready GraphQL API to any existing Spring Boot application in an afternoon.

hook.real_world_problem:
Flipkart REST API explosion:

```
150 REST endpoints for the product catalog:
  GET /products, GET /products/{id}
  GET /products/{id}/reviews, GET /products/{id}/images
  GET /products/{id}/seller, GET /products/{id}/shipping
  GET /categories, GET /categories/{id}/products
  GET /search?keyword=&category=&minPrice=&maxPrice=&sort=...
  ... (142 more)

Problem:
  Mobile team: 'I need these 3 fields from product + seller name only'
  Backend: 'Our endpoints don't support partial responses'
  Mobile: 'OK I'll just ignore the other 200KB of JSON'
  Backend: 'Please stop DDoSing us with unnecessary data'

With Spring for GraphQL:
  One new file: src/main/resources/graphql/schema.graphqls
  One new annotation: @QueryMapping, @MutationMapping
  Mobile gets: exactly the 3 fields it needs
  No new endpoints. Same @Service, @Repository beans.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Flipkart product catalog GraphQL API implemented in Spring Boot with @QueryMapping resolvers and @GraphQlTest.

concept_explanation.setup:
**Spring for GraphQL setup:**

```xml
<!-- pom.xml -->
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-graphql</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>  <!-- HTTP transport -->
</dependency>
```

```yaml
# application.yml
spring:
  graphql:
    graphiql:
      enabled: true  # http://localhost:8080/graphiql (browser playground)
    schema:
      locations: classpath:graphql/  # where to find .graphqls files
```

visual_aid.description: Spring for GraphQL architecture. Left: HTTP client sends POST /graphql with query body. Center-left: Spring Web layer receives request. Center: GraphQL Engine (parses query, validates against schema). Center-right: @Controller with @QueryMapping methods (product, products, searchProducts) and @SchemaMapping (Product.reviews, Product.seller). Right: @Service and @Repository beans (existing Spring beans, no changes needed). Bottom: schema.graphqls file (in resources/graphql/) being read at startup. Annotation: 'Same @Service beans as REST — just new @Controller annotations'.
visual_aid.alt_text: Spring for GraphQL: @QueryMapping connects schema to Spring @Controller methods

worked_example.scenario: Implement Flipkart product catalog GraphQL resolvers.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. schema.graphqls: Product, Category, Query, Mutation types
2. @Controller ProductGraphQlController with @QueryMapping product() and @MutationMapping createProduct()
3. @SchemaMapping for Product.reviews (field resolver)
4. @GraphQlTest: test product query returns correct data

step_by_step_solution.details: @QueryMapping: shorthand for top-level query fields (type Query). @SchemaMapping: for all other type fields. Product.reviews is a field resolver — called once per Product in the result set (N+1 risk — solved by DataLoader in T3). When a client queries { product { reviews { rating } } }, Spring calls product() first, then reviews(product) for each product returned.

things_to_notice: @Argument maps query arguments to Java parameters. For simple scalars: @Argument String id. For complex input types: @Argument CreateProductInput input — Spring auto-deserializes the GraphQL input map to the Java record/class.
@GraphQlTest: loads only the GraphQL layer (controllers + schema) — much faster than @SpringBootTest. Use GraphQlTester to execute queries and assert on the result.
Method name matters: @QueryMapping method named 'product' maps to schema Query.product. If names differ: @QueryMapping(name='product') public Product getProduct(...) — explicitly specify the mapping.

Null return from @QueryMapping: if product() returns null, GraphQL response has `data: { product: null }`. This is correct for 'not found' — not an exception. Throw an exception only for server errors.

industry_examples.use_case: Spring Boot REST API coexists with new GraphQL API on same application. Payment @Service beans reused unchanged. New @Controller with @QueryMapping payment(id), @QueryMapping payments(merchantId, status), @MutationMapping initiateRefund(input). Dashboard web app uses GraphQL for complex nested queries; webhook integrations still use REST. Both /api/payments (REST) and /graphql coexist on port 8080.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How would you add GraphQL to our existing Spring Boot app?' → spring-boot-starter-graphql dependency, schema.graphqls in resources/graphql/, @Controller with @QueryMapping/@MutationMapping methods. Existing @Service beans reused unchanged. @SchemaMapping for nested field resolvers. @GraphQlTest for testing. GraphiQL enabled for development. N+1 problem: @BatchMapping or DataLoader (T3). No architecture rewrite needed — additive change.
applications.real_world_workflow: Spring for GraphQL integration: (1) add starter dependency, (2) create schema.graphqls matching existing domain model, (3) add @Controller with @QueryMapping methods delegating to existing @Service, (4) add @SchemaMapping for nested fields (reviews, seller), (5) enable GraphiQL for team exploration, (6) @GraphQlTest for each resolver, (7) monitor: slow field resolvers → add DataLoader.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Java method annotated with @QueryMapping returns null, but schema declares the field as non-null (Product!). Spring for GraphQL propagates this as an error.
common_gaps.remediation: Schema field type and Java return type must match nullability. If schema says Product! (non-null), never return null — throw a GraphQlException if not found. If schema says Product (nullable), returning null is correct for 'not found'. Rule: nullable in schema = may return null; non-null in schema = must return non-null or throw.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @QueryMapping returns Optional<Product> or null for a schema field declared as Product! (non-null) — GraphQL engine propagates null upward, potentially nulling out the parent type

key_takeaways: @QueryMapping: maps method to Query type field (method name = field name by default)
@MutationMapping: maps method to Mutation type field
@SchemaMapping(typeName='X', field='y'): maps method to field y on type X
@Argument: extracts query argument into Java parameter (auto-deserializes input types)
GraphiQL: spring.graphql.graphiql.enabled=true (browser UI for query testing)
@GraphQlTest: slice test for GraphQL layer (loads controller + schema only)
Method name = schema field name: @QueryMapping product() → Query.product
Null return: nullable field → returns null in response; non-null field → error (don't return null)
Existing @Service: unchanged — @Controller delegates to @Service (same pattern as REST)

self_check_questions.question: When should you use @SchemaMapping vs @QueryMapping, and what's the N+1 risk with @SchemaMapping?
self_check_questions.answer: @QueryMapping: top-level Query type fields only — `product(id)`, `products()`, `searchProducts()`. @SchemaMapping: field resolvers on non-Query types — `Product.reviews`, `Product.seller`, `Order.items`. N+1 risk: when a query returns a list of products and each product has @SchemaMapping for 'reviews', Spring calls reviews() ONCE PER PRODUCT. 10 products → 10 reviews() calls → 10 database queries. Example: client queries 10 products with reviews → 1 query for products + 10 queries for reviews = 11 total (N+1). This is why T3 covers DataLoader: DataLoader batches all 10 reviews() calls into ONE database query: SELECT * FROM reviews WHERE product_id IN (1,2,3,...10). Rule: if a field resolver (Product.reviews) will be called for many parent objects, use @BatchMapping or DataLoader to avoid N+1.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: DataLoader — solving the N+1 query problem in GraphQL. When 10 products each load their reviews, DataLoader batches all 10 into a single database query.
